topic: Flagship new topic: causal identification for compositional treatments on a simplex. Characterize when a reported 'effect of increasing component j' is a well-defined causal estimand: because treatment shares sum to one, every component increase requires a substitution rule over the remaining components. The proposed kernel is an exact reallocation-invariance theorem: a component-effect functional is nonparametrically identified from observational or randomized mixture data if and only if all admissible substitution paths have the same path derivative of the dose-response surface; otherwise only path-specific reallocation effects are identified. Include a finite three-component witness where two substitution paths have opposite signed component effects despite the same observed mixture-response law, plus a sharp simplex-gradient condition recovering standard continuous-treatment dose-response as the unconstrained limit. Position against continuous-treatment causal dose-response and compositional-data regression, not as an upgrade of any banked topic.

open_problems.open_problem: Characterize necessary and sufficient conditions under which an 'effect of increasing component j' on the simplex is invariant to the reallocation rule over the remaining components.

source_refs.claim: The paper distinguishes total and relative causal effects for compositional data and says fixed-total analyses require careful interpretation of joint effects.
source_refs.author: Arnold, Berrie, Tennant & Gilthorpe

source_refs.claim: The paper formalizes direct substitutions, inadvertent substitutions with several components, and average relative causal effects as distinct estimands.
source_refs.author: Tomova, Gilthorpe & Tennant

source_refs.claim: The mixture-model note compares component-slope, Scheffe, and Cox parameterizations, making component slopes direction/reference dependent.
source_refs.author: Piepel

open_problems.why_unsolved: Compositional-causal papers explicitly separate total, relative, direct-substitution, inadvertent-substitution, and average-relative effects, while mixture-experiment parameterizations treat slopes as tied to directions or references; none gives an iff condition under which all admissible substitution directions collapse to one component estimand.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to report one component coefficient fails because Arnold et al. and Tomova et al. identify relative/substitution effects as joint interventions whose target changes with the component or mixture of components being displaced.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: An exact identification theorem on the simplex tangent cone, with finite-contrast and local-gradient clauses, stating precisely when all admissible reallocations have the same causal derivative and otherwise proving that only path-specific substitution effects are identified.

open_problems.open_problem: Develop support-local exact and partial identification theory for compositional component effects when some substitution paths have empirical support and others require extrapolation.

source_refs.claim: Generalized propensity-score methods identify continuous-treatment dose-response functions under weak unconfoundedness and support/overlap conditions.
source_refs.author: Hirano & Imbens
source_refs.venue: Applied Bayesian Modeling and Causal Inference from Incomplete-Data Perspectives

source_refs.claim: For air-pollution mixtures, estimands of initial interest can correspond to mixture values not represented in observed data, motivating alternatives less reliant on extrapolation.
source_refs.author: Antonelli & Zigler

source_refs.claim: Multivariate mixture interventions face positivity violations that can leave deterministic interventions unidentified or dependent on unreliable extrapolation.
source_refs.author: Huang, Dong, Lin & Antonelli

open_problems.why_unsolved: Continuous-treatment dose-response theory is written for scalar or Euclidean doses with overlap conditions, whereas mixture papers emphasize that multicomponent interventions may move to unsupported joint mixtures; the missing object is a simplex-specific support rule distinguishing identifiable tangent directions from unsupported substitution policies.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to apply a continuous-treatment dose-response formula fails because Antonelli and Zigler and Huang et al. name positivity and extrapolation as central obstacles for joint mixture values, and simplex reallocations require support for the whole counterfactual composition rather than for one coordinate alone.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A sharp support-local theorem that point-identifies reallocation derivatives only over empirically admissible simplex directions and gives sharp bounds or a nonidentification witness for unsupported substitution rules.

open_problems.open_problem: Classify compositional substitution rules as treatment versions and determine when averaging over versions yields a well-defined component estimand rather than a policy-indexed effect.

source_refs.claim: The paper provides identification results when treatment has multiple versions and notes that effects can be defined by specifying version distributions.
source_refs.author: VanderWeele & Hernan

source_refs.claim: The dietary-substitution paper identifies average causal effects only after a hypothetical substitution strategy is specified.
source_refs.author: Chiu & Wen

source_refs.claim: Longitudinal modified treatment policies target practical interventions such as reducing or increasing exposure by a specified amount and derive an identifying formula for the specified policy.
source_refs.author: Diaz
source_refs.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association

open_problems.why_unsolved: Multiple-version and modified-treatment-policy work resolves identification after a version distribution or policy is specified, and dietary substitution work does the same for a named substitution strategy; it does not supply an equivalence criterion for when different substitution versions should be treated as the same component effect.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to average over an arbitrary reallocation distribution fails because VanderWeele and Hernan show that version distributions define different effects, while Chiu and Wen identify a specified substitution strategy rather than an invariant component effect.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A version-equivalence theorem for simplex interventions, proving that two reallocation policies define the same component estimand exactly when they induce the same potential-outcome contrast, with a sensitivity region when plausible version distributions differ.

open_problems.open_problem: Derive efficient influence functions and debiased estimators for simplex-gradient causal effects, including both rule-specific and rule-invariant weighted average derivative effects.

source_refs.claim: Average derivative effects use local overlap for continuous treatments, but standard estimation has required conditional-density ingredients; the paper derives density-free efficient estimators for selected ADEs.
source_refs.author: Hines, Diaz-Ordaz & Vansteelandt

source_refs.claim: The paper develops efficient semiparametric estimation for multivalued treatment effects under ignorability, not for singular simplex-valued continuous shares.
source_refs.author: Cattaneo

source_refs.claim: Log-contrast mixture models impose zero-sum coefficient restrictions and express effects through log-ratio contrasts on the simplex.
source_refs.author: Aitchison & Bacon-Shone

open_problems.why_unsolved: Average-derivative and semiparametric-efficiency results for continuous or multivalued treatments do not handle the singular law induced by shares summing to one, while compositional log-contrast models provide transformations and coefficient constraints rather than causal tangent-space influence functions.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to use Euclidean average derivative effects fails because Hines et al. require continuous-treatment derivatives under local overlap, while Aitchison and Bacon-Shone's log-contrast setup makes clear that simplex shares live under a zero-sum constraint and cannot vary coordinatewise freely.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A tangent-space Riesz representation and efficient influence function for simplex-valued treatments, with boundary-aware local overlap conditions and a testable gradient-equality restriction certifying reallocation invariance.

open_problems.open_problem: Construct sharp partial-identification and sensitivity-analysis bounds for high-dimensional compositional treatments with unmeasured confounding and substitution-direction targets.

source_refs.claim: The overlap paper formalizes why overlap assumptions become stronger and more restrictive as covariate dimension grows.
source_refs.author: D'Amour, Ding, Feller, Lei & Sekhon

source_refs.claim: Multi-treatment causal effects with unobserved confounding are partially identified, and Bayesian conclusions can be highly sensitive to parameterization and priors.
source_refs.author: Zheng, D'Amour & Franks

source_refs.claim: Multi-cause causal inference can fail under unobserved confounding, motivating explicit sensitivity and partial-identification analysis rather than automatic deconfounding.
source_refs.author: D'Amour

open_problems.why_unsolved: High-dimensional overlap and multi-treatment partial-identification results show that simultaneous-treatment causal claims can be weakly supported and sensitive to parameterization, but they do not exploit simplex geometry or target substitution-direction contrasts among shares.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to treat compositional shares as ordinary simultaneous treatments fails because D'Amour et al. show overlap deteriorates in high dimension and Zheng et al. show multi-treatment partial-identification conclusions can depend strongly on parameterization and priors.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: Sharp bounds for directional simplex effects under explicit sensitivity restrictions on unobserved confounding, invariantly stated in tangent or log-ratio coordinates and accompanied by overlap diagnostics for bound width.

literature_map:
Scheffe's mixture-experiment tradition and later Cox/Piepel parameterizations model responses as functions of proportions, so a component slope is inherently tied to a direction or reference composition.
Aitchison and Bacon-Shone's log-contrast model makes the zero-sum structure explicit through log-ratio contrasts rather than unconstrained coordinate effects.
Arnold, Berrie, Tennant and Gilthorpe (2020, IJE) give the central causal warning: compositional effects are joint effects and analysts must state which causal effect is sought.
Tomova, Gilthorpe and Tennant (2022, AJCN) separate direct substitutions, inadvertent substitutions involving several components, and average relative causal effects; these are distinct estimands.
Chiu and Wen (2025, Statistics in Medicine) identify effects for dietary substitution strategies only after the hypothetical substitution intervention is specified.
VanderWeele and Hernan (2013, JCI) supply the multiple-versions lens: version distributions define different causal effects, matching the ambiguity of reallocation rules.
Hirano and Imbens (2004) and Cattaneo (2010, Journal of Econometrics) cover continuous or multivalued treatment identification/efficiency, but not singular simplex-valued treatments.
Hines, Diaz-Ordaz and Vansteelandt (2026, Biometrika) make average derivative effects attractive under local overlap, yet their derivatives are not formulated on a simplex tangent space.
Antonelli and Zigler (2024, AJE) show that environmental-mixture estimands can require extrapolation to unsupported mixture values and propose alternatives tied to observed support.
Huang, Dong, Lin and Antonelli (2026, arXiv) extend incremental interventions to multivariate mixtures and frame positivity violations as a primary obstacle for deterministic interventions.
D'Amour, Ding, Feller, Lei and Sekhon (2021, Journal of Econometrics) formalize high-dimensional overlap fragility; compositional shares inherit this issue through joint support on a lower-dimensional simplex.
Zheng, D'Amour and Franks (2022, AISTATS) show multi-treatment causal effects under unobserved confounding are partially identified and parameterization sensitive, motivating invariant simplex-coordinate sensitivity analysis.
The live tension is therefore not whether a named substitution policy can be identified, but when all policies that increase one component define the same causal estimand.
prior_proposal_map:
Track A mined doc/formalization/_bank candidates, downgraded, failed, and legacy proposal/reviewer artifacts; no prior proposal directly studied compositional treatments on a simplex.
pid_dose_response_iv/v1 supplied the closest continuous-treatment neighbor: local derivative signs can depend on cross-dose coupling rather than marginal dose-response information.
pid_dose_response_shape/v1 and pid_continuous_iv_no_monotonicity/v1 supplied adjacent partial-ID templates where pointwise or dosewise relaxations fail to paste into a joint latent law.
pid_did_anticipation_bounded/v1 supplied a finite shared-coordinate/opposite-sign template, but it is panel-specific and was not merged into the simplex ledger.
eid_shift_share_design/v1 and pid_rdd_manipulation_bounded/v1 were share/budget adjacent but concern IV/RD designs rather than compositional treatment effects.
Study-pipeline gaps on LATE, Manski bounds, and interval regression were reviewed as augmentation candidates; they were not emitted because they do not satisfy the topic-specific simplex reallocation anchor.
